steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank must complete a logical inference. The key facts: (1) neither Geoffrey of Monmouth’s History of the Kings of Britain nor any work predating it mentions the Round Table; (2) Malory’s Le Morte d’Arthur does include it. Since many of Malory’s sources derive from Geoffrey, but the Round Table appears nowhere in Geoffrey’s tradition, the blank must explain where Malory found it.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Geoffrey of Monmouth's accounts of Arthurian legends in *History* are more similar overall in content to the accounts in *Perceval, the Story of the Grail* than they are to the accounts in *Le Morte d'Arthur*.’: The passage never compares Geoffrey to Perceval; this comparison isn’t supported. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Malory encountered the Round Table in a source that Geoffrey of Monmouth was not familiar with when writing his *History*.’: This fits exactly — if Geoffrey’s sources lack the Round Table and Malory has it, Malory must have drawn from a source outside Geoffrey’s tradition. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘*Le Morte d'Arthur* is more historically accurate than *History*, because *Perceval, the Story of the Grail* had not been written when Geoffrey of Monmouth was writing his work.’: Historical accuracy is never discussed; and the reasoning given (that Perceval hadn’t been written yet) doesn’t follow from the evidence. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘when a version of an Arthurian legend contradicted the version in *History*, Malory preferred to include Geoffrey of Monmouth's version in *Le Morte d'Arthur*.’: This contradicts the evidence — Malory included the Round Table, which is absent from Geoffrey’s version. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
